title: Reddit announces $1B share repurchase program as quarterly earnings top estimates
detail:
Reddit announces $1B share repurchase program as quarterly earnings top estimates
Proactive
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 7:56 AM PST 1 min read
In this article:
RDDT
Reddit announces $1B share repurchase program as quarterly earnings top estimates Proactive uses images sourced from Shutterstock

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) has reported its fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results, posting earnings and revenue that exceeded Wall Street expectations and unveiling a $1 billion share repurchase program.

For Q4, Reddit reported earnings per share of $1.24, surpassing analysts’ consensus of $0.94, while revenue reached $726 million, well above the forecast of $577 million.

Revenue grew 70% year-over-year, driven primarily by advertising, which rose 75% to $690 million. Other revenue contributed $36 million, up 8% from the prior year.

Daily Active Uniques (DAUq) globally totaled 121.4 million, a 19% increase from the prior year, slightly above analyst expectations of 120 million.

Net income for the quarter was $252 million, up 255% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA reached $327 million.

For the full year 2025, Reddit generated $2.2 billion in revenue, up 69% from 2024, with net income of $530 million and adjusted EBITDA of $845 million. Gross margins were 91.2% for the year.

Reddit also guided Q1 revenue of $595 million to $605 million, above the $577 million consensus estimate, and adjusted EBITDA of $210 million to $220 million.

In addition to reporting strong financial results, Reddit’s board of directors authorized a share repurchase program of up to $1 billion of Class A common stock.

“We’re entering the next era of Reddit—defined by sharper execution, global expansion, and product innovation that puts real people and conversations at the center,” Reddit CEO Steve Huffman said in a statement. “Our focus is on turning Reddit’s authenticity into even more everyday utility.”

Shares of Reddit fell 3.4% following the report, likely reflecting profit-taking.

title: Why Analysts Say The Reddit RDDT Story Is Shifting With AI And Valuation Risks
detail:
Why Analysts Say The Reddit RDDT Story Is Shifting With AI And Valuation Risks
Simply Wall St
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 6:08 AM PST 5 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Find winning stocks in any market cycle. Join 7 million investors using Simply Wall St's investing ideas for FREE.

Why Reddit’s Price Target Just inched Higher

Reddit’s fair value estimate has been nudged from US$251.19 to US$252.39 per share, a small move that still reflects how quickly the story around growth, monetization, and AI is evolving. Recent research has focused on whether Reddit can lift revenue per user and turn AI and data licensing into a more meaningful revenue stream, leading to a slightly higher revenue growth assumption and a marginally lower discount rate in updated models. Stay with this article to see how you can keep on top of these shifting assumptions and the changing Reddit stock narrative over time.

Analyst Price Targets don't always capture the full story. Head over to our Company Report to find new ways to value Reddit.

What Wall Street Has Been Saying

🐂 Bullish Takeaways

Several firms are constructive on Reddit's long term growth potential, with Evercore ISI starting coverage with an Outperform rating and a US$320 price target, and Argus lifting its target to US$300 from US$250.

Evercore ISI points to a three year revenue CAGR of 30% to 40% and EBITDA CAGR of 40% to 50% as the basis for viewing the current valuation as robust but still reasonable given its outlook.

Wells Fargo and Morgan Stanley both raised targets in January 2026, to US$207 from US$186 and to US$265 from US$250 respectively, tying their views to steady execution, product work around onboarding and AI search, and potential benefits from data licensing deals.

Cantor Fitzgerald and Argus highlight Reddit's differentiated position and what they see as a strong user and advertiser proposition, with Cantor describing the platform as a leading digital media asset with network effects, even while taking a Neutral stance at a US$240 target.

🐻 Bearish Takeaways

A cluster of recent reports on 6 February 2026 from Oppenheimer, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley and Piper Sandler all cut their Reddit price targets, signaling a more cautious tone after earlier optimism.

Where details are available, firms such as Cantor Fitzgerald and Wells Fargo flag valuation and current pricing as key reservations, with Cantor saying Reddit's strong recent stock performance already reflects a bullish outlook and Wells Fargo pointing to softer app traffic data as a watch item.

Even some of the more constructive analysts, like Wells Fargo, frame 2026 as a key year with important execution tests around AI search and renewals of data licensing deals. This adds an element of timing and delivery risk to the story.

The mix of higher targets in January followed by target cuts in early February underlines how sensitive Reddit's valuation is to shifts in assumptions about growth durability, user trends and the monetization ramp from GenAI related opportunities.

title: I'm 37, Have $600K Invested, and Just Got $300K — Should I Pay Off My Mortgage or Invest?
detail:
I'm 37, Have $600K Invested, and Just Got $300K — Should I Pay Off My Mortgage or Invest?
AJ Fabino
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 6:01 AM PST 4 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Benzinga and Yahoo Finance LLC may earn commission or revenue on some items through the links below.

Quick Summary

A 6.5% mortgage is a meaningful drag on long-term wealth, but wiping it out can also limit flexibility.

If you're facing similar decisions, getting a free independent review through SmartAsset can help clarify fees and long-term tradeoffs.

For those diversifying into income-producing assets Arrived can offer a way to add real estate starting with as little as $100.

At 37, having more than $600,000 invested already puts one Reddit user well ahead of most Americans their age. But adding a sudden $300,000 cash windfall can complicate things fast.

That's especially true when there's still a $420,000 mortgage on the books at 6.5%, daycare bills every month, and no firm retirement timeline. In that situation, the question is about deciding where risk actually belongs in your life over the next 20 to 30 years.

A guaranteed 6.5% return from paying down debt looks attractive. So does the long-term growth potential of staying fully invested. What makes this hard is that both choices are reasonable, and both create different vulnerabilities.

Here's how people in this position usually think through the trade-offs.

Start by Getting Clarity on the Trade-Offs

When people inherit or receive a large sum, one of the smartest first moves is getting a financial advisor's perspective through a free tool like SmartAsset. The biggest risk isn't picking the wrong fund, it's making a permanent decision in a vacuum.

Mortgage rates, tax treatment, retirement accounts, childcare costs, and future income growth all interact in ways that aren't obvious on a spreadsheet. Paying down debt can feel safe, but it can reduce flexibility. Staying fully invested can look optimal on paper, but it increases exposure to bad timing.

That's why many people in this position start by getting an outside perspective.

Take a short quiz with SmartAsset to compare financial advisors who work with families balancing investing, debt, and long-term planning. Seeing different strategies side by side often makes the real trade-offs clearer.

When assets are growing and responsibilities are growing at the same time, clarity becomes a form of protection.

The Appeal and Risk of Paying Down the Mortgage

With a 6.5% fixed mortgage and access to tools like a Rocket Mortgage HELOC in the background, some homeowners in this position start by looking hard at what paying down debt actually buys them.

From a math perspective, sending extra cash to principal delivers a risk-free benefit. Every dollar reduces future interest and lowers required monthly payments. That added breathing room can matter when you are juggling childcare costs, career uncertainty, and long-term planning.

title: Girlfriend, 25, Refuses To Co-Sign Car Loan After Boyfriend Blows Savings On Sneakers—His Mom Sends Guilt Trip Text 'That's What Partners Do'
detail:
Girlfriend, 25, Refuses To Co-Sign Car Loan After Boyfriend Blows Savings On Sneakers—His Mom Sends Guilt Trip Text 'That's What Partners Do'
Jeannine Mancini
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 5:01 AM PST 5 min read
In this article:
RDDT

There's supporting your partner—and then there's financially adopting them.

That's the line one 25-year-old woman found herself toeing when her 27-year-old boyfriend asked her to co-sign a car loan. He needed a vehicle for work, he said. What he didn't mention right away was that he had the down payment saved—until he blew it on a PlayStation 5, designer sneakers, and a weekend trip with his friends.

In a post on Reddit, she explained her refusal. "I told him no I'm not risking my credit when he just proved he can't manage his own," she wrote. His response? "He said I'm unsupportive and don't believe in his potential," she said. Then came the kicker: "Now his mom even texted me saying I should help him because that's what partners do."

Don't Miss:

Missed Nvidia and Tesla? RAD Intel Could Be the Next AI Powerhouse — Just $0.85 a Share

Americans With a Financial Plan Can 4X Their Wealth — Get Your Personalized Plan from a CFP Pro

The poster admitted that the guilt was starting to creep in. "I'm starting to feel guilty," she wrote, "but also I'm not a bank." Still, she questioned whether she was wrong for saying no.

A Liability, Not A Partner

Reddit had answers—and none of them involved her signature.

"His ‘potential' means nothing if his choices are a PS5 and sneakers over reliable transportation," one commenter wrote. "He's not a partner; he's a financial liability. The fact that his mommy is texting you to pressure you is a massive red flag."

Another added, "If his own mama won't even co-sign for him, why the heck would you? Don't do it girl!"

Users weren't just concerned about her credit—they were waving flags about the relationship itself. "She needs to end the relationship now! Him and his mother have given her every reason to prove this is a very unhealthy relationship," one commenter wrote. "If she stays, she pays."

Others pointed out the absurdity of the emotional manipulation. "Believing in his potential doesn't mean signing legal documents for someone who just blew his savings on luxury items. He doesn't need a co-signer. He needs accountability."

Trending: Motley Fool's analysts have built a new lineup of passive ETFs — explore which "Foolish" strategy fits your investment goals.

On The Hook For A Car You Don't Drive

From a financial perspective, co-signing isn't just a gesture of support. It's a legal contract that makes both parties equally responsible for repayment. If he stops paying—or never starts—the lender comes after the co-signer. Late payments hit both credit scores. Collections hit both names. And in the event of a breakup, the debt doesn't vanish just because the relationship does.

title: Is Waiting Until Age 70 to Claim Social Security Benefits the Best Financial Move?
detail:
Is Waiting Until Age 70 to Claim Social Security Benefits the Best Financial Move?
Waiting until you turn 70 to claim Social Security will get you a higher monthly benefit, but it can be tough to bridge the gap until then.

10'000 Hours / Getty Images

Kristina Byas
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 3:44 AM PST 3 min read
In this article:
RDDT
Key Takeaways

Waiting until age 70 to claim Social Security benefits maximizes the amount of your monthly payments.

Monthly benefits can be up to 132% higher when claiming at age 70 compared with claiming early at age 62.

Retirement savings, pensions, and part-time income can bridge the gap to make the delay financially feasible.

Deciding when to start taking Social Security can be a financial planning challenge. It requires everyone to weigh their individual circumstances—their salary, their retirement savings and other investments, their expenses, how long they're able to work, and more—to determine the best strategy.

More from Yahoo Scout

How can you bridge income gap before 70?

How much more can you earn waiting until 70?

What factors should influence your claiming decision?

What are delayed retirement credits and how work?

A post on Reddit captured the dilemma perfectly. The user wrote:

“I am interested in people’s thoughts. It seems lots of people are advising to wait till age 70 to claim Social Security for the one that has the higher level of benefits. Particularly if the spouse will go for spousal benefits. Are you waiting to claim as late as possible? Are you withdrawing from your 401k/IRA, or ... do you have an employer pension? Or are you claiming when you retire?”

This question resonates because when you take your Social Security benefits can affect your Social Security income for the rest of your life. While it's a great idea to put off your claim for as long as possible so that you get the biggest monthly payment available, some people may not want or be able to do that. However, for many workers and retirees, delaying benefits is one of the most powerful long-term financial strategies available.

Waiting Until 70 Can Pay Off Big

Social Security is designed with built-in incentives for delaying your claim. While you can start collecting as early as age 62, doing so comes with a permanent reduction in the amount of your monthly benefit, often around 30% less than what you’d receive at your full retirement age (FRA), which is 67 if you were born in 1960 or later.

But the real magic happens between your FRA and age 70. This is when you have the greatest potential to increase your benefits.

For every year you delay past your FRA, Social Security awards Delayed Retirement Credits, which increase your benefit by up to 8% per year, depending on your birth year. That can translate to roughly 132% of your full benefit if you wait until age 70 rather than claim early at 62. Think of it as receiving a guaranteed return, something that’s increasingly difficult to achieve in low-risk investments.

Let’s say you're about to retire, and your FRA benefit is $2,000 per month. There's an 8% increase in benefits for every year you delay.

Story Continues

title: Helping Your Child Decide: Are Student Loans Worth It?
detail:
Helping Your Child Decide: Are Student Loans Worth It?
When considering student loans, try to balance big dreams with long-term financial stability.

GCShutter / Getty Images

Dara-Abasi Ita
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 3:37 AM PST 3 min read
In this article:
RDDT
Key Takeaways

When evaluating whether your child should take on student debt, try to understand their dreams while planning for their financial reality.

If your child does decide to take out loans, suggest that they not borrow more than what they'll make their first year out of college.

Encourage them to consider attending a community college for the first two years of their degree.

Discuss the possibility of taking a gap year, which would give them time to build up savings.


A parent posted on Reddit's StudentLoans forum a question that probably keeps thousands of families up at night: their child wants to take out student loans for their dream school, and they're unsure what to tell them.

More from Yahoo Scout

Should students choose federal or private loans?

How much student debt is too much?

How can families reduce college costs effectively?

What are alternatives to expensive four-year universities?

This is a tricky question because student debt can feel like such a burden. The typical student's average student loan debt may be as high as $42,673. And the damage goes beyond bank accounts. In one survey, 78.7% of respondents said they’ve experienced anxiety due to their student loan debt. One in 16 respondents had suicidal thoughts because of their loans, and for borrowers who were unemployed or made less than $50,000, it was one in eight.

​Nobody wants to be the parent who says no to their kid's dreams. But nobody wants their kid drowning in debt, either.

A Few Rules to Remember

Financial planners have a guideline: your total student loans shouldn't exceed your first-year salary.

So if an engineering grad lands a job making $78,000 per year (which is, on average, about what an engineer just out of college will make), they can handle loan payments on up to $78,000 of debt.

On the other hand, a communications grad making $60,000 per year (again, the average salary for a recent college grad with that major) would feel overwhelmed if they took out $80,000 in student loans, because the monthly payment would be more than what's considered manageable (8% to 10% of a new grad's gross income).

Note

For the 2025-26 school year, undergraduate federal loans have an interest rate of 6.39%, while graduate loans are 7.94% and parent and grad PLUS loans are 8.94%. Private loans can be anywhere from 3.39% to 17.99%.

​It's typically best to go federal, rather than private, if you're taking out student loans. Federal loans' interest rates are fixed, while private loans' interest rates can be fixed or variable. Plus, with a federal student loan, you have the option to do an income-driven repayment plan. From a parent's perspective, private loans can be risky. Private loans need co-signers over 90% of the time, and many of those co-signers—usually parents—wind up making the payments.

title: Reddit Buyback Signals Confidence As Earnings And Volatility Shape Investor Debate
detail:
Reddit Buyback Signals Confidence As Earnings And Volatility Shape Investor Debate
Simply Wall St
Fri, February 6, 2026 at 3:06 AM PST 3 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Track your investments for FREE with Simply Wall St, the portfolio command center trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) announced a surprise $1b share repurchase program alongside its fourth quarter results.

The buyback marks the company’s first major share repurchase since going public.

The move comes after recent share price weakness, with the stock at $151.05.

For investors watching NYSE:RDDT, the timing of this $1b buyback comes after a sharp pullback in the stock, with shares down 20.5% over the past week and 40.5% over the past month. Year to date, Reddit is down 37.6%, and 30.2% over the past year, despite the latest quarter coming in strong enough for management to pair earnings with this new authorization.

This combination of a sizeable repurchase plan and recent volatility creates an important moment for investors tracking how Reddit handles capital allocation as a newly public tech name. The buyback size and pace, together with future earnings updates, will likely shape how the market interprets management’s conviction in the current valuation and long term growth plans.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Reddit by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Reddit.

NYSE:RDDT 1-Year Stock Price Chart

Why Reddit could be great value

Reddit’s first major buyback as a listed company comes on the back of a very profitable year, with Q4 2025 net income of US$251.6m and full year net income of US$529.72m after a prior year loss, which gives management meaningful earnings power to support capital returns. For investors, pairing a US$1b repurchase authorization with guidance for Q1 2026 revenue of US$595m to US$605m and a recent share price pullback frames this as both a signal of confidence in the business and a statement about how the board currently views the share price.

How This Fits Into The Competing Reddit Narratives

The buyback and strong earnings print feed directly into the more optimistic Reddit narrative that focuses on advertising, data licensing and operating leverage. This suggests the company is starting to convert its large user base into profits more effectively, even as it competes with Meta, Alphabet and Snap for ad dollars. At the same time, the bearish narrative around governance, regulatory pressure and dependence on user generated content does not disappear here, so some investors may read this move as management leaning into their control structure to actively manage the equity story while the business model matures.

title: RDDT Q4 Deep Dive: Ad Platform Expansion and User Engagement Fuel Growth
detail:
RDDT Q4 Deep Dive: Ad Platform Expansion and User Engagement Fuel Growth
RDDT Q4 Deep Dive: Ad Platform Expansion and User Engagement Fuel Growth
Jabin Bastian
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 9:32 PM PST 6 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Online community and discussion platform Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) beat Wall Street’s revenue expectations in Q4 CY2025, with sales up 69.7% year on year to $725.6 million. On top of that, next quarter’s revenue guidance ($600 million at the midpoint) was surprisingly good and 4.3% above what analysts were expecting. Its GAAP profit of $1.24 per share was 33.1% above analysts’ consensus estimates.

More from Yahoo Scout

What's driving Reddit's user engagement improvements?

What drove Reddit's strong Q4 revenue growth?

How is Reddit planning capital allocation strategies?

How are AI-powered ad tools impacting performance?

Is now the time to buy RDDT? Find out in our full research report (it’s free).

Reddit (RDDT) Q4 CY2025 Highlights:

Revenue: $725.6 million vs analyst estimates of $667.5 million (69.7% year-on-year growth, 8.7% beat)

EPS (GAAP): $1.24 vs analyst estimates of $0.93 (33.1% beat)

Adjusted EBITDA: $327 million vs analyst estimates of $286.5 million (45.1% margin, 14.1% beat)

Revenue Guidance for Q1 CY2026 is $600 million at the midpoint, above analyst estimates of $575.2 million

EBITDA guidance for Q1 CY2026 is $215 million at the midpoint, above analyst estimates of $204.9 million

Operating Margin: 31.9%, up from 12.4% in the same quarter last year

Domestic Daily Active Visitors: 52.5 million, up 4.5 million year on year

Market Capitalization: $28.62 billion

StockStory’s Take

Reddit delivered quarterly results that exceeded Wall Street’s expectations, with the market responding positively to broad-based revenue growth and improving profitability. Management attributed the outperformance to ongoing investments in machine learning-driven ad formats and product enhancements, particularly around user onboarding and search integration. CEO Steven Huffman emphasized that “streamlining onboarding does improve retention,” and credited improved feed relevancy and search capabilities for driving user engagement and monetization. COO Jennifer Wong highlighted that the advertising business saw substantial gains across all verticals, with performance ads and shopping formats contributing meaningfully to growth.

Looking ahead, Reddit’s guidance reflects confidence in scaling automation and personalization initiatives designed to expand advertiser adoption and boost engagement. Management pointed to advancements in AI-powered ad tools, such as Reddit Max, and ongoing enhancements to the shopping ad ecosystem as key levers for sustaining revenue momentum. CFO Andrew Vollero noted the company’s focus on deploying capital efficiently, balancing continued investment in core business, M&A opportunities, and a newly authorized $1 billion share repurchase program. CEO Steven Huffman added, “Making Reddit faster, more relevant, and more accessible to everyone, everywhere, is our top priority as we execute our 2026 roadmap.”

title: Reddit Inc (RDDT) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Highlights: Record Revenue and User Growth Propel ...
detail:
Reddit Inc (RDDT) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Highlights: Record Revenue and User Growth Propel ...
GuruFocus News
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 9:03 PM PST 4 min read
In this article:
RDDT

This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Revenue: $2.2 billion for the full year, up 69% year over year; Q4 revenue of $726 million, up 70% year over year.

Net Income: $530 million for the full year; Q4 net income of $252 million, 35% of revenue.

Adjusted EBITDA: $845 million for the full year, 38% margin; Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $327 million, 45% of revenue.

Free Cash Flow: $264 million in Q4, 36% of revenue; full year free cash flow of $684 million.

Daily Active Users (DAU): 121 million in Q4, up 19% year over year.

Weekly Active Users (WAU): 471 million in Q4, up 24% year over year.

Advertising Revenue: $690 million in Q4, up 75% year over year.

Gross Margin: 91.2% for the full year, up 70 basis points.

Average Revenue Per User (ARPU): $5.98 in Q4, up 42% year over year.

Stock-Based Compensation: 13% of revenue in Q4.

Share Repurchase Program: $1 billion authorized with no set expiration date.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 4 Warning Sign with AMZN.

Is RDDT fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

Release Date: February 05, 2026

For the complete transcript of the earnings call, please refer to the full earnings call transcript.

Positive Points

Reddit Inc (NYSE:RDDT) reported a significant revenue increase, reaching $2.2 billion for 2025, up 69% year over year.

The company achieved a net income of $530 million, demonstrating strong profitability.

Daily active users grew by 19% year over year, reaching 121 million, indicating robust user engagement.

Reddit Inc (NYSE:RDDT) announced a $1 billion share repurchase program, reflecting confidence in its financial health.

The advertising business saw a 75% year-over-year growth in Q4, driven by strong performance across various channels and verticals.

Negative Points

The company faces challenges in distinguishing human-generated content from AI-generated content, which could impact user trust.

There is a need for further improvement in user onboarding and retention to sustain growth.

Reddit Inc (NYSE:RDDT) is still in the early stages of optimizing its shopping ad solutions, requiring more work on machine learning and adoption.

The company plans to phase out reporting on logged-in versus logged-out users, which may affect transparency in user engagement metrics.

Despite strong growth, Reddit Inc (NYSE:RDDT) still has significant headroom to penetrate large customer segments and expand its lower funnel advertising.

Q & A Highlights

Q: Why is Reddit opting for a share buyback instead of investing in future growth and product initiatives? A: Andrew Vollero, CFO: The decision to initiate a share buyback is part of a strategic move to return capital to shareholders while still prioritizing investment in the business and potential M&A opportunities. With $2.5 billion in cash, Reddit can pursue all three capital priorities: investing in the business, M&A, and share repurchases.

title: Reddit (RDDT) Valuation Check As Shares Lag And DCF Views Sharply Diverge
detail:
Reddit (RDDT) Valuation Check As Shares Lag And DCF Views Sharply Diverge
Simply Wall St
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 8:30 PM PST 4 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Find your next quality investment with Simply Wall St's easy and powerful screener, trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Reddit (RDDT) has drawn attention after a period of weak share performance, with the stock showing negative returns over the past week, month and past 3 months. This has prompted fresh questions about how investors are valuing its business.

See our latest analysis for Reddit.

At a share price of US$151.05, Reddit’s recent momentum has weakened, with a 7 day share price return of 20.52% and a year to date share price return of a 37.55% decline. The 1 year total shareholder return is 30.23% lower, signaling fading enthusiasm compared with earlier trading this year.

If this pullback has you reassessing growth focused names, it could be a good moment to scan a curated list of 56 profitable AI stocks that aren't just burning cash for other ideas in the broader tech space.

So with Reddit shares sliding and the stock trading at a reported 62% discount to one intrinsic value estimate, should you see this weakness as a fresh entry point, or assume the market is already pricing in the company’s future growth?

Most Popular Narrative: 297.5% Overvalued

Reddit closed at $151.05, while the most followed narrative on the stock, according to Goran_Damchevski, points to a fair value near $38 using a discounted cash flow approach with a 7.1% discount rate.

Reddit reported revenues of $348M in Q3’24, up 68% YoY. Analysts expected $312.8M, while I forecasted a CAGR of 21%, implying quarterly revenues above $275M. The company clearly beat those expectations and the market bid up the price by more than 20% on the next day’s open.

Read the complete narrative.

Want to see why such a low fair value still leans on strong revenue growth, rising margins and a future profit multiple more common in mature tech names? The tension between early profitability, heavy stock based compensation and ambitious long term earnings assumptions sits at the heart of this story. Curious how those moving parts combine into that $38 outcome while analysts as a group sit higher? The full narrative lays out the numbers in detail.

Result: Fair Value of $38 (OVERVALUED)

Have a read of the narrative in full and understand what's behind the forecasts.

However, that story can change quickly if Reddit struggles to improve advertiser returns on its platform, or if regulatory scrutiny limits the potential of its data licensing deals.

Find out about the key risks to this Reddit narrative.

Another View: SWS DCF Points The Other Way

While the popular narrative pegs Reddit’s fair value at US$38 and labels the stock overvalued, our DCF model presents a different perspective. At a share price of US$151.05, Reddit is trading at a 62% discount to an estimated future cash flow value of US$397.43. Is the gap a warning about optimistic growth assumptions, or a sign the market is being too harsh on the story right now?

title: Compared to Estimates, Reddit Inc. (RDDT) Q4 Earnings: A Look at Key Metrics
detail:
Compared to Estimates, Reddit Inc. (RDDT) Q4 Earnings: A Look at Key Metrics
Zacks Equity Research
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 3:30 PM PST 2 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Reddit Inc. (RDDT) reported $725.61 million in revenue for the quarter ended December 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 69.7%. EPS of $1.24 for the same period compares to $0.36 a year ago.

The reported revenue represents a surprise of +8.71% over the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $667.48 million. With the consensus EPS estimate being $0.96, the EPS surprise was +29.3%.

While investors closely watch year-over-year changes in headline numbers -- revenue and earnings -- and how they compare to Wall Street expectations to determine their next course of action, some key metrics always provide a better insight into a company's underlying performance.

As these metrics influence top- and bottom-line performance, comparing them to the year-ago numbers and what analysts estimated helps investors project a stock's price performance more accurately.

Here is how Reddit Inc. performed in the just reported quarter in terms of the metrics most widely monitored and projected by Wall Street analysts:

DAUq (Daily Active Uniques) - Global: 121.4 million compared to the 120.04 million average estimate based on eight analysts.

ARPU - Global: $5.98 versus the eight-analyst average estimate of $5.59.

DAUq (Daily Active Uniques) - U.S.: 52.5 million versus the seven-analyst average estimate of 52.05 million.

DAUq (Daily Active Uniques) - International: 68.9 million compared to the 67.99 million average estimate based on seven analysts.

ARPU - U.S.: $10.79 versus $10.05 estimated by six analysts on average.

ARPU - International: $2.31 versus $2.02 estimated by six analysts on average.

Logged-in DAUq - International: 27.7 million versus 28.27 million estimated by four analysts on average.

Logged-in DAUq - Global: 50.7 million versus the four-analyst average estimate of 51.65 million.

Logged-in DAUq - U.S.: 23 million versus 23.39 million estimated by four analysts on average.

WAUq - Global: 471.6 million versus 456.07 million estimated by three analysts on average.

Revenue- International: $142.3 million versus the six-analyst average estimate of $139.58 million. The reported number represents a year-over-year change of +77.9%.

Revenue- United States: $583.3 million versus the six-analyst average estimate of $529.94 million. The reported number represents a year-over-year change of +67.8%.

View all Key Company Metrics for Reddit Inc. here>>>

Shares of Reddit Inc. have returned -40.2% over the past month versus the Zacks S&P 500 composite's +0.5% change. The stock currently has a Zacks Rank #4 (Sell), indicating that it could underperform the broader market in the near term.

title: Reddit looks to AI search as its next big opportunity
detail:
Reddit looks to AI search as its next big opportunity
Sarah Perez
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 3:20 PM PST 3 min read
In this article:
RDDT
Image Credits:TechCrunch

Reddit suggested on Thursday that its AI-powered search engine could be the next big opportunity for its business — not just in terms of product, but also as a revenue driver impacting its bottom line. During the company’s fourth-quarter earnings call on Thursday, it offered an update on its plans to merge traditional and AI search together and hinted that although search is not yet monetized, “it’s an enormous market and opportunity.”

In particular, the company believes that generative AI search will be “better for most queries.”

“There’s a type of query we’re, I think, particularly good at — I would argue, the best on the internet — which is questions that have no answers, where the answer actually is multiple perspectives from lots of people,” said Reddit CEO Steve Huffman.

Traditional search, meanwhile, is more like navigation — it’s a way to find the right link to a topic or subreddit. But LLMs can be good at this, too, if not better, he said. “So that’s the direction we’re going.”

The exec also noted that weekly active users for search over the past year grew 30% from 60 million users to 80 million users. Meanwhile, the weekly active users for the AI-powered Reddit Answers grew from 1 million in the first quarter of 2025 to 15 million by the fourth quarter.

“We’re seeing a lot of growth there, and I think there’s a lot of potential too,” Huffman added.

Reddit said it’s working to modernize the AI answers interface by making its responses more media-rich, and pilots of this are already underway.

The company is also thinking about how it can position itself when it’s not just a social site, but a place people come for answers. Reddit told investors on the call that it’s doing away with the distinction between logged-in and logged-out users starting in Q3 2026, as it will aim to personalize the site — using AI and machine learning — and make it relevant to whoever shows up.

The company announced in 2025 it was planning to combine its AI search feature, Reddit Answers, with its traditional search engine to improve the experience for end users. In the fourth quarter, Reddit said it had made “significant progress” in unifying its core search and its AI feature. It also released five new languages on Reddit Answers and is piloting dynamic agents along with search results that include “media beyond text.”

Though Reddit sees value in its AI answers, it’s not been keeping that to itself. The company’s content licensing business, which allows other companies to train their AI models on its data, is growing, too. That business revenue is reported as part of Reddit’s “other” revenues (i.e., its non-ad revenue). This “other” revenue increased by 8% year-over-year to reach $36 million in Q4 and was up 22% to reach $140 million for 2025.

title: Reddit (RDDT) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript
detail:
Reddit (RDDT) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript
Motley Fool Transcribing, The Motley Fool
Thu, February 5, 2026 at 3:06 PM PST 55 min read
In this article:
RDDT

Image source: The Motley Fool.

Date

Feb. 5, 2026 at 4:30 p.m. ET

Call participants

Chief Executive Officer — Steven Huffman

Chief Operating Officer — Jennifer Wong

Chief Financial Officer — Andrew Vollero

Chief Product Officer — Maria Angella Dew Smith

Need a quote from a Motley Fool analyst? Email pr@fool.com


Full Conference Call Transcript

Steven Huffman: Thanks, Jesse. Hi, everyone, and thanks for joining our Q4 earnings call. 2025 was a breakout year for Reddit, Inc. Surpassed both targets, built real momentum across our business, improved our unique community model at scale. Crossed $2.2 billion in revenue, up 69% year over year, and delivered $530 million in net income. In Q4 alone, we welcomed over 121 million daily active users to our platform, up 19% year over year and over 471 million weekly active users, up 24%. None of this would be possible without our team. Thank you to our employees for a phenomenal year.

Momentum we're seeing across the business, especially in all three sections of our ad funnel, is the result of years of foundational work coming to life. I know we are all eager to build on this success. The numbers tell just a small part of an important story. Reddit, Inc. is at the center of a once-in-a-generation shift. And it's not a coincidence. We're now operating in a fundamentally different Internet. One shaped by opaque algorithms, generative content, and growing distrust. And yet, amid this shift, more people are turning to Reddit, Inc. Not just to aimlessly scroll, to connect, learn, and research. That's because Reddit, Inc. is the most human place on the Internet.

In a world flooded with AI swap, people are seeking real community, lived experience, and trusted opinions. That's Reddit, Inc.'s differentiator. To put it simply, more people than ever are coming to Reddit, Inc. because Reddit, Inc. is for everyone. One of the main reasons Reddit, Inc. is the go-to place for community is the candor of our conversations. This authenticity is rare. And it's what makes conversations on Reddit, Inc. uniquely helpful and influential. But in the age of AI, you can't easily distinguish a real person's thoughts or recommendations from a bot that trust erodes. That's why we're actively working on ways to preserve our authenticity and conversation quality.

Then we'll quickly move to bot verification and labeling next. This begins the launch of verified profiles for brands and individuals in Q4. We're making good progress here, and we're excited to share more updates in the coming weeks. Our consumer product work remains a top priority. Particularly driving user growth, retention, and deeper engagement through more seamless experiences. Two areas of especially high priority improving new user onboarding and integrating our search interfaces. Let's dig into how these are going. On the onboarding side, we deployed numerous experiments in Q4. We're actively iterating on these learnings and working fast to improve retention for new and casual users. That retention supports growth, engagement, monetization, and more effective marketing.
